---
title: Custom Sources — Ingesting from HTTP, APIs, and Beyond
---

Every pipeline so far has read from somewhere the library can *list*: a directory of
Parquet files, or another Delta table queried through its Change Data Feed.
The watermark tracks a file path or a table version, and "what's new?" is answered by
diffing that list against what has already been seen.

A lot of data does not arrive that way.
It sits behind an HTTP endpoint that serves a fresh CSV every morning, or an API whose
download URLs change on every publish, or a service that hands you the whole history and
leaves you to work out what changed.
For these, "which file have I seen?" is the wrong question.
The right one is "what cursor did I leave off at?" — and that cursor might be a timestamp,
a month stamp, an ETag, or a set of URLs.

The `Source` protocol exists so you can answer that question however your transport demands,
without giving up the watermarking, idempotency, and lazy-transform contract from the
previous posts.

## The protocol

A source is any object with a single method:

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Source(Protocol):
    def poll(self, since: object | None) -> Batch | None:
        ...
```

`poll` receives the cursor saved by the previous run — `None` on the first run — and returns
either a `Batch` (new data plus the next cursor) or `None` when nothing has changed:

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Batch:
    frame: pl.LazyFrame
    cursor: object        # JSON-serialisable; handed back as `since` next run
```

That is the whole contract.
Everything downstream is unchanged: the pipeline runs your transform on `batch.frame`, writes
the target, and only then persists `batch.cursor`.

The cursor is stored in the *same* watermark table the file-based sources use, serialised as
JSON. The write-before-watermark ordering from the [watermarks post](02-watermarks-idempotent-ingestion.qmd)
applies identically — if the process dies after the target write but before the cursor is
saved, the next run re-polls from the old cursor and re-writes the same batch.
An upsert target (`merge_on`) absorbs the repeat, so you still get at-least-once delivery with
idempotent results.

Because the cursor stores JSON rather than a file path or a version integer, the watermark
schema differs from the built-in sources.
A pre-existing watermark written by a file-based pipeline is simply ignored on the first custom
run, which starts from `since=None` as if fresh.

Most of the time you will not write `poll` by hand. Two helpers cover the common shapes.

## `from_query` — a column high-water-mark

The most common HTTP pattern is a source that hands you a snapshot and expects you to figure
out what is new.
`from_query` reduces that to one decision: name a column, and its maximum value becomes the
cursor for the next run.

```python
from data_warehousing_with_polars import incremental, from_query


def fetch_orders(since: str | None) -> pl.LazyFrame | None:
    resp = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/orders.csv")
    df = pl.read_csv(resp.content)
    if since is not None:
        df = df.filter(pl.col("updated_at") > since)
    if df.is_empty():
        return None
    return df.lazy()


@incremental(
    source=from_query(fetch_orders, cursor_on="updated_at"),
    target="/data/delta/orders",
    merge_on="order_id",
)
def orders(lf: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.LazyFrame:
    return lf
```

On the first run `since` is `None`, so `fetch_orders` returns the whole table and the cursor
becomes the largest `updated_at` it saw.
On the next run that value comes back as `since`, the function filters to newer rows, and the
cursor advances again.
When the filter leaves nothing, the function returns `None` and the run is a no-op — no target
write, no cursor change.

There is one deliberate trade-off.
To compute the cursor max, `from_query` collects the returned frame once:

```python
def poll(self, since: object | None) -> Batch | None:
    lf = self._fn(since)
    if lf is None:
        return None
    df = cast(pl.DataFrame, lf.collect())
    cursor = df[self._cursor_on].max()
    if cursor is None:
        return None
    return Batch(frame=df.lazy(), cursor=cursor)
```

That `collect()` is fine for HTTP and API payloads that fit in memory, which is the case it is
built for. If your source is large enough that collecting it is a problem, you have outgrown
`from_query` — implement `Source` directly and compute the cursor without materialising
everything.

### The snapshot-replacement variant

Some endpoints give you no `updated_at` to filter on.
They republish the *entire* history every period, identical rows and all — the Munich
Monatszahlen datasets work exactly like this.
Here the natural cursor is not a column in the data but the period itself.
Stamp the batch with the current period and use that stamp as the cursor:

```python
def fetch_month(since: str | None) -> pl.LazyFrame | None:
    stamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m")
    if since == stamp:
        return None                       # already ingested this month
    df = pl.read_csv(download_current_csv())
    return df.with_columns(pl.lit(stamp).alias("_stamp")).lazy()


@incremental(
    source=from_query(fetch_month, cursor_on="_stamp"),
    target="/data/delta/monatszahlen",
    merge_on=["category", "month"],
)
def monatszahlen(lf: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.LazyFrame:
    return lf.drop("_stamp")             # cursor column is not part of the data
```

The `since == stamp` check short-circuits the download entirely once the month is done, so a
pipeline scheduled hourly does the actual fetch at most once per month.
`merge_on` collapses the duplicate history within each delivery, and the transform drops
`_stamp` before it reaches the target — that column exists only to drive the cursor.

## `from_frame` — full refresh every run

For a small reference table you simply want to re-read in full each time — a lookup of country
codes, a feature-flag table, a slowly-changing dimension small enough not to bother with deltas
— `from_frame` skips the cursor logic entirely:

```python
from data_warehousing_with_polars import incremental, from_frame


@incremental(
    source=from_frame(lambda: pl.scan_csv("https://example.com/reference.csv")),
    target="/data/delta/reference",
    merge_on="code",
)
def reference(lf: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.LazyFrame:
    return lf
```

The cursor is always `None`, so every run ingests everything.
That makes `merge_on` mandatory in practice: without it, each run would append a second copy of
the whole table.
With it, the merge updates rows in place and the target converges to the latest snapshot no
matter how many times you run it.

## Implementing `Source` directly

When the cursor is neither a column max nor "always refresh", write the protocol yourself.
The motivating case is a paginated or multi-file API where "what's new" is a *set of URLs* you
have not downloaded yet — there is no high-water-mark column, because the unit of progress is
the URL, not a row value.

```python
from data_warehousing_with_polars import incremental
from data_warehousing_with_polars.incremental import Batch


class ApiFileSource:
    """Cursor is the sorted list of already-downloaded URLs."""

    def poll(self, since: object | None) -> Batch | None:
        seen: set[str] = set(since) if isinstance(since, list) else set()
        available = list_download_urls()              # ask the API what exists
        new = [u for u in available if u not in seen]
        if not new:
            return None
        frame = pl.concat([pl.scan_csv(download(u)) for u in new])
        return Batch(frame=frame, cursor=sorted(seen | set(new)))


@incremental(
    source=ApiFileSource(),
    target="/data/delta/api_files",
    merge_on="id",
)
def api_files(lf: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.LazyFrame:
    return lf
```

The cursor here is a JSON list that grows by one entry per file ingested.
Because it is stored in the watermark and handed back as `since`, the source only ever
downloads URLs it has not seen — across process restarts and machine migrations, since none of
that state lives locally.

One caution, because it is the thing that can bite you: the cursor is serialised to JSON and
written to the watermark on every run, so an unbounded set of URLs means an ever-growing cursor.
For a source that tops out at a few hundred files this is a non-issue.
If yours is genuinely unbounded, encode progress more compactly — a single "max date seen" or
"last page token" — rather than accumulating every identifier.

## Fanning in: every source is just a `Source`

The protocol pays a second dividend beyond HTTP and APIs.
Once *every* source — a directory of files, a Delta table read through CDF, a custom poller —
is reduced to the same `poll(since) -> Batch | None` shape, fan-in stops being a special case.
Internally a directory becomes a source whose cursor is the set of processed paths, and a Delta
table becomes one whose cursor is the last version; a user `Source` already is one.

So you can pass a **list** to `source` and the pipeline polls each slot with its own cursor,
omits the ones with nothing new, and hands the rest to the transform as positional arguments:

```python
@incremental(
    source=[
        "/data/uploads/",                              # directory source
        from_query(fetch_orders, cursor_on="updated_at"),  # HTTP source
    ],
    target="/data/delta/combined",
    merge_on="id",
)
def combine(*lfs: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.LazyFrame:
    return pl.concat(list(lfs))
```

Each slot keeps an independent cursor in the watermark, so a run where only the HTTP source has
moved processes just that frame and leaves the directory's cursor untouched.
Use `*lfs` rather than fixed parameters, since idle sources drop out of the argument list — the
same variable-arity rule the file-only fan-in already followed.

The one combination a single list cannot express is directories *and* Delta tables together,
because `file_format` is a pipeline-wide setting. If you need that mix, wrap one of them in a
custom `Source`.

## Choosing among the three

The decision is short:

- The source exposes a column that monotonically marks freshness (`updated_at`, a sequence
  number, a period stamp)? **`from_query`** with that column as `cursor_on`.
- The source is a small full table you want to re-merge every run? **`from_frame`** with
  `merge_on`.
- Progress is tracked by something that is not a column — a set of URLs, an ETag, a page token?
  **Implement `Source`** and put that something in the cursor.

In all three, the rest of the pipeline is unchanged.
The transform still receives a `LazyFrame` and knows nothing about where the data came from.
The watermark still records progress only after a successful write.
Idempotency still falls out of at-least-once polling combined with an upsert target.
The protocol changes only *how the pipeline learns what is new* — never the guarantees it makes
once it knows.
